claim: Psilocybin (and psilocyn) are Schedule I controlled substances, codified among hallucinogenic substances at 21 CFR 1308.11(d) with DEA code 7437.
scheduling_action: Codified Schedule I hallucinogen, 21 CFR 1308.11(d) (original CSA placement 1971)
reason: No currently accepted medical use in the US, lack of accepted safety under medical supervision, high abuse potential (Schedule I hallucinogen criteria).
pending_status: No federal rescheduling; active FDA Breakthrough-designated clinical research + state programs (e.g. Oregon) do not change the federal Schedule I listing.
program_impact: Schedule I is a major research/handling constraint for psilocybin CNS programs (DEA Schedule I registration, quota) — a hurdle to surface, not a veto.
source: 21 CFR 1308.11(d), Schedule I hallucinogenic substances (1996 codified edition), GPO govinfo

claim: Lysergic acid diethylamide (LSD) is a Schedule I controlled substance, codified among hallucinogenic substances at 21 CFR 1308.11(d) with DEA code 7315.
scheduling_action: Codified Schedule I hallucinogen, 21 CFR 1308.11(d) (original CSA placement 1971)
reason: No currently accepted medical use in the US, lack of accepted safety under medical supervision, high abuse potential (Schedule I hallucinogen criteria).
program_impact: Schedule I imposes the most restrictive research/handling controls on this CNS hallucinogen.
source: 21 CFR 1308.11(d), Schedule I hallucinogenic substances (1996 codified edition), GPO govinfo

claim: MDMA is a Schedule I controlled substance, codified among hallucinogenic substances at 21 CFR 1308.11(d) with DEA code 7405.
scheduling_action: Codified Schedule I hallucinogen, 21 CFR 1308.11(d); DEA permanently placed MDMA in Schedule I by 1988 final rule after Grinspoon v. DEA litigation
reason: DEA found high abuse potential and no accepted medical use under its criteria; reaffirmed Schedule I in 1988 after the First Circuit vacated the initial placement.
pending_status: Context (not from the cited CFR primary; per FDA + widespread reporting): in Aug 2024 FDA issued a Complete Response Letter to Lykos Therapeutics declining MDMA-assisted PTSD therapy and requesting another Phase 3 trial — so MDMA remains Schedule I with no FDA-approved product.
program_impact: Schedule I + no approved product = research-only controlled status; a live regulatory-landscape item for CNS/PTSD programs.
source: 21 CFR 1308.11(d), Schedule I hallucinogenic substances (1996 codified edition), GPO govinfo

claim: DEA placed ketamine, including its salts and isomers, into Schedule III of the Controlled Substances Act effective August 12, 1999.
scheduling_action: DEA final rule placing ketamine (incl. salts, isomers, salts of isomers) into Schedule III
reason: Over 775 DEA reports of ketamine diversion/abuse since 1992 (chiefly from veterinary clinics); PCP-like but less intense pharmacology; HHS recommendation; met the three Schedule III criteria.
pending_status: None; stable in Schedule III since 1999.
program_impact: Establishes the Schedule III base from which esketamine (an isomer) is controlled by reference.
source: Federal Register final rule, 64 FR 37673 (July 13, 1999), GPO govinfo
quote: places the substance ketamine, including its salts, isomers, and salts of isomers, into schedule III of the Controlled Substances Act

claim: Esketamine (Spravato) is a Schedule III controlled substance, controlled by reference as the (S)-isomer of ketamine rather than by a separate DEA scheduling action.
current_schedule: Schedule III (by isomer-reference; codified at 21 CFR 1308.13(c) as a ketamine isomer)
scheduling_action: No standalone esketamine rule; controlled automatically as an isomer of ketamine under the 1999 ketamine Schedule III final rule (which controls 'isomers')
reason: Esketamine is the (S)-enantiomer of ketamine; the 1999 ketamine rule controls ketamine 'including its salts, isomers, and salts of isomers'. FDA approved Spravato March 2019 for treatment-resistant depression.
pending_status: None on scheduling; marketed under a REMS for sedation/dissociation/abuse risk.
program_impact: Shows controlled status can attach by isomer-reference without a drug-specific action — constrains a marketed CNS antidepressant.
source: Federal Register ketamine final rule, 64 FR 37673 (July 13, 1999) — controlling 'isomers' — GPO govinfo (no esketamine-specific FR; see manifest gap)
quote: places the substance ketamine, including its salts, isomers, and salts of isomers, into schedule III of the Controlled Substances Act

claim: Under the 2000 GHB final rule, GHB is Schedule I in bulk while an FDA-approved GHB drug product (sodium oxybate / Xyrem) is Schedule III (subject to Schedule I criminal sanctions).
current_schedule: Schedule III (FDA-approved GHB products); Schedule I for all other GHB material
scheduling_action: DEA final rule adding GHB to Schedule I and placing FDA-approved GHB drug products in Schedule III
reason: Public Law 106-172 (Hillory J. Farias and Samantha Reid Date-Rape Drug Prohibition Act of 2000); HHS recommendation; documented overdoses/deaths and date-rape misuse. The statute split control: Schedule I for the substance, Schedule III for FDA-approved products.
pending_status: None; sodium oxybate/Xyrem remains Schedule III under a restricted-distribution REMS.
program_impact: Classic split-schedule outcome — the same molecule is Schedule I in bulk but Schedule III as an FDA-approved CNS drug.
source: Federal Register final rule, 65 FR 13235 (Mar 13, 2000), GPO govinfo
quote: If or when a drug product containing GHB is approved by the FDA under this section, it shall be a Schedule III controlled substance except that it will be subject to the criminal sanctions applicable to a Schedule I controlled substance, pursuant to Public Law 106-172.

claim: DEA rescheduled FDA-approved synthetic dronabinol capsules (Marinol) from Schedule II to Schedule III effective July 2, 1999.
current_schedule: Schedule III (FDA-approved synthetic dronabinol in sesame oil / soft gelatin capsules)
scheduling_action: DEA final rule transferring the FDA-approved synthetic dronabinol capsule product from Schedule II to Schedule III
reason: HHS/FDA scientific and medical evaluation (Sept 11, 1998) and recommendation; lower abuse potential than Schedule I/II with accepted medical use.
pending_status: Originally formulation-specific (capsules); a 2010 rule (75 FR 67054) broadened it to FDA-approved generic dronabinol capsules, and a 2017 rule placed FDA-approved dronabinol ORAL SOLUTION (Syndros) in Schedule II.
program_impact: Illustrates formulation-specific scheduling — different FDA-approved dronabinol products can sit in different schedules (III capsules vs II oral solution).
source: Federal Register final rule, 64 FR 35928 (July 2, 1999), GPO govinfo
quote: the Deputy Administrator transfers from schedule II to schedule III of the CSA the drug containing synthetic dronabinol [(-)- 9-(trans)-tetrahydrocannabinol] in sesame oil and encapsulated in soft gelatin capsules

claim: In September 2018 the DEA placed certain FDA-approved cannabidiol drug products (Epidiolex) in Schedule V of the Controlled Substances Act.
current_schedule: Schedule V as of the 2018 order (FDA-approved CBD drugs); see pending_status for the later Farm-Bill effect
scheduling_action: DEA final order placing certain FDA-approved cannabidiol drug products in Schedule V
reason: FDA approval of Epidiolex (June 25, 2018) for Lennox-Gastaut and Dravet seizures; HHS evaluation found very low abuse potential; placement was the least-restrictive schedule consistent with Single Convention obligations.
pending_status: Subsequently overtaken: FDA-approved CBD with <0.1% THC was effectively removed from CSA control following the 2018 Agriculture Improvement Act's hemp redefinition — verify current status separately before relying on Schedule V.
program_impact: Demonstrates the FDA-approval-to-DEA-scheduling pathway for a cannabis-derived CNS drug; a notable scheduling change for a CNS therapeutic.
source: Federal Register final order, 83 FR 48950 (Sept 28, 2018), GPO govinfo
quote: places certain drug products that have been approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and which contain cannabidiol (CBD) in schedule V

claim: Effective April 28, 2026, DEA/DOJ placed FDA-approved drug products containing marijuana in Schedule III of the Controlled Substances Act (down from Schedule I).
current_schedule: Schedule III for FDA-approved marijuana products (botanical marijuana itself remains Schedule I unless FDA-approved)
scheduling_action: DEA/DOJ final rule + final order rescheduling FDA-approved marijuana products from Schedule I to Schedule III
reason: HHS medical and scientific evaluation (Aug 2023) supporting Schedule III; obligations under the Single Convention on Narcotic Drugs (1961) per 21 U.S.C. 811(d)(1).
pending_status: Final order issued/effective April 28, 2026, with corresponding permit-requirement changes.
program_impact: Major 2026 scheduling shift relevant to cannabis-derived CNS therapeutics; reduces controls on FDA-approved marijuana products to Schedule III.
source: Federal Register final rule/final order (Apr 28, 2026), GPO govinfo
quote: places drug products containing marijuana that have been approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in schedule III of the Controlled Substances Act
